label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's see. The question is asking what happens when they're looking for a missing child according to the passage. First, I need to read the passage again carefully. The passage starts by listing the steps taken when a child is missing: putting an APB, contacting hospitals, calling parents, friends, relatives, checking local and national papers, putting posters, going on TV, and mentioning a magazine with photos of missing children. Then it says, "Of course, with nearly two million missing children every year, we can't do all these things for everyone. We haven't got the time, or the money, or the people who work for it." So the key point here is that there are many methods listed, but due to the high number of missing children, they can't apply all the methods to every case. That means they might prioritize or not be able to use all the listed approaches for each individual case. Looking at the options: A) can always find him - The passage doesn't say they can always find the child. It just talks about the procedures, not the success rate. B) couldn't always try every way mentioned above - This seems to align with the part where it says "we can't do all these things for everyone." So they can't use every method for each case. C) just try one way or another - The passage lists multiple methods, but the key is that they can't do all for each case, not that they only use one method. D) won't give up until they find him - The passage doesn't mention giving up or persistence; it's about
